speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three. And the survivor is Mr. Victor Dortheimer. Before the tape break you were talking about Amon Goeth, and how he was-- what he was like in the Plaszow-- in the Plaszow camp. What were the sleeping conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sleeping, this was, I-- like, wait a second. You know what is barracks? And this was double bed, one floor. You know what this is, one floor? One and another one. How you call in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: Levels? Different levels?

speaker: interviewee
text: In every-- in every bed was two people. That means we'd been sleeping like the sardines. My head and his feet was here, and vice versa. Was very cold. We haven't got the blanket. Just nothing. It was very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we somehow organized from the cement factory, there was cement-- the socks. So we would cover with the socks, with the pipers on. This was the condition. And what other clothes were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: From beginning, everyone from ghetto came with the best clothes. And when we came, it was an order from authority of SS, because they were afraid that somebody can run away. So this was local people what was working in camp, and it was people outside were living-- were-- working.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the outside people went every day to this work and return. And suddenly the-- the order was to paint everyone outside with yellow stripes, what was-- with paint, paint, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just stripes, jacket and trousers. So from 500 meter, you saw there are prisoner. And the inside people was a red stripes. This was the condition. So if you saw with a red strip or yellow strip you'll know exactly where they are working.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the purpose was that nobody could-- can run away, because immediately they know their place. Yes? Did anyone try to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyone, they tried, but 90% they caught and hanged them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any form of spiritual resistance or religious resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Religious? It was-- I haven't been there, but I know from people, there was some very religious Jews in our camp. And especially they wanted to pray, come Yom Kippur. And they knew, the German, they knew. They got agents between-- even between Jews. And they knew the Yom Kippur. And they caught these people. I don't know how many, a group, 20, 30, I don't know. And they shot them. This was religious. For example, I saw when we came-- when we-- when we were working in villas Goeth, they brought from ghetto a group of rabbis with wives, with children. It was not far away because, the grate was always open. And we saw the group, they took off everything, and they're naked. They shot them. And with me, especially with me, was an elderly man, also good painter. And he was religious. And when he saw this picture, how they're shooting the rabbis, they start to tear his hair from his head. And said no, no-- no. [CRYING] Excuse me. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was trying to be quiet. It's all right. Let me put this back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, God doesn't exist for him. Before he was praying every day in the morning, he stopped praying. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This was the Goeth. Ask me, ask me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given a number when you were in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long-- we haven't got a number, because we didn't exist for the authority. The number-- if you had number, you'll be a number. If they shot you, they shoot-- this is what the German order. They should register this and this-- the number died because he was cold, or the-- you know. But we didn't exist. How long we haven't got number, we didn't exist. The number we get in Gross-Rosen, when we went from Plaszow, transferred to Schindler, we'd been-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] recognized as a number, what they need to produce arms. So this time they couldn't shoot us without reason. This was the German order.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came to Gross-Rosen, every one of us got a number. And my number, I remember, was 69124. And the women, they get number in Auschwitz. And when were you taken to Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: After we liquidated the factory, we sent everything away. And the Emalia was empty. So we should go to factory to work to Sudentenland. So to transfer us from Schindler factory, to the main camp-- what is Plaszow-- this is the official way, this is-- our-- the-- because we were under control of the Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Plaszow, from Plaszow to Gross-Rosen. Register into Schindler. If I go back to the first Schindler factory you were in, what was its full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The official name was Deutsche Emailwarem Fabrik. Can you write?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can spell it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a piece of paper. And I can spell it. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They call short, DEF-- Deutsche-- you want to write? D-E-U-T-S-C-H-E, Emailwarem E-M-A-I-L-W-A-R-E-M, Fabrik F-A-B-R-I-K.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was DEF. This was the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please?

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was it? How large was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was approximately-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] together with the Polish people, maybe, 900-1,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? For '43, May, until October '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you were a painter. And what type of jobs did you do in the factory, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a lot of officers. Because their officers, this was a factory they built up. That Schindler built up. And everything was new. And it was officers, maybe 50 rooms. And big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lunch. And big salle for dancing and banquet. And now, I had machine, and a huge rooms for the machines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything should be painted, because it's going to be rusted. And did the painting ever finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have-- I have people. I was the boss. But I had five, six, eight, 10 people. It's up to what I need. It was a big job. I had free hands to take on other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the people that you took on? Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [DRINKS] [NON-ENGLISH]. But not everyone came to Brunnlitz-- to Sudetenland. Not everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this was-- we didn't need so many. So the Schindler left just 300 people, the best, the best men and wives. And the rest, the rest they sent to Plaszow, because he didn't need. This was-- and the trick-- the 400 that went from us they sent to Mauthausen. I don't know. They-- I don't know how many survived from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Helena, your wife, doing when she was-- was she with you in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was working with email-- the castors, you know-- all very well kept.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the women and men separated, or were they working together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, together, together. But just as in the barracks, there was women barracks and men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you ever see Helena at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night and not day, because I was free. I had my room-- my-- the work room. Beautiful room, and it-- painting and everything there. And Helena, her and I wanted, she came. Because in factory, we'd been free.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your room like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your room like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Room?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Half of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So quite a big room.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you have inside it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Painting and brushes and ladders and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you sleep in that room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no-- the-- I was sleeping in the barracks. It was five minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you sleep with everybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep with everyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, wasn't-- with my wife?

speaker: interviewer
text: With your-- with you other male work colleagues.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were two. No, one, one. Everyone had one bed at Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everyone had their own bed at Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Own bed, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Including the women? Did they have their own separate beds? And did you have blankets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what clothes were you wearing when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The stripes, that we go from SS, stripes. They took the-- they-- when we went out, they took us, the striped, the painted clothes. And they gave us their clothes with the stripes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what food did you eat when you were in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I eat? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a private question because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [LAUGHING] I have-- I wasn't depend about food from my barracks, because everyone getting in barracks, soups. This was in factory, Polish kitchen. I had the privilege to get my meal in Polish food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you have the privilege?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I don't know. From Schindler. Did you get on well with him? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what food were you eating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sausage, good soup, piece of meat?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did Helena eat the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is long story. I'm going to explain you how I get very well. But it's not for here, not here. Private, I cannot tell you, because it's too long story. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've got the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't say it in a shortened version.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. These clothes-- this is short story. These clothes, what we were painting, they took. They brought us from SS the stripe. and this was lying, that's lying. And the manager, the policeman-- the Jewish policeman, in the camp, by Schindler-- he was already my good friend. I said, excuse me, give me one jacket. I want-- I like to clean. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he said. Take. It was beautiful jacket. And it-- it cost everything. Beautiful, new. So I took one jacket.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a painter, I know how to get rid of the paint. And I was trying, with paint pen and turpentine. But for producing arms, we need spatial-- how you can say-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like you wanted to clean the nails from the paint. How you call that? Remover. Nail polish remover. Acetone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Remover. Paint remover. But this paint remover was strictly for the arms. But it was a director, it was vice director, was German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to vice director, because I was good with him, excuse me, engineer Fuchs, I need spatial work. I need to have this. And he was strict, you know, a Nazi. What for? I said this is spatial.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, exception, I give you, but no more. Forgive me one detail. And I tried. Like miracle, gone. Wow, gone, the whole paint gone. And I had Polish friend who I was working with, and I sold them for food. And they were, happy, and a good price. OK, and the little gone. A few jackets, and that-- I'm going through this engineer Fuchs, and the engineer Fuchs like, I'm going to call gestapo. You are saboteur, this is just for am.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said excuse me, OK, OK, OK. So I went to director Schindler, just director and him, the director, I need-- I need remover. What for? I say to him, director, I tell you-- I'm cleaning jackets and trousers and I'm selling. So, I give you little, no more.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] And this was my-- this was my job. After another thing in the-- in the-- in the camp, was a Jewish policeman, but in the camp, not with me. Victor, I need beautiful the castle you're painting. I said OK, give me food. Yeah. Because [LAUGHS] everything in factory, through that Schindler manage everything. I was very well, very well. And Schindler-- Schindler-- for Schindler, I need a bread. Come on, take a bread and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever sit and have a meal with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we drank vodka together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just the two of you or were there others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just two. He came in the night, little bit drunk, as usual, [LAUGHS] without exception-- no girl. Because otherwise he's always with girl. [DRINKS] He said, come here with me. I said, well-- To my room. I go-- beautiful room. Lunch, and beautiful bedroom and beautiful bar, and liters of--

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going to drink together. It was the night. A bottle of vodka, we're going to drink. I said, director, what do you want? He said, listen, you are not stupid. You know that the Polish stealing my email. I said, yes. But you know who? I said, yes, but I'm not going to tell you. Why? Because I am not your agent. If you respect me as a painter, OK, I've done everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again and again. And he said, you are-- you are the honest man. I have enough-- enough agents. OK, this was Schindler. I don't know. He loves me. I love him.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example, was a period in factory that the porters-- they were German, Polish-German-- and they got a, you know the room in the front? They control everybody who's going out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, it was '43, was very-- I don't know, the Polish and everybody want could kill the Jews, or beat the Jews. OK. The Jews was like rats. It was one engineer, Lambert, was my engineer, mine. Because I was a painter, it was an engineer. And this, he was a drinker, anti-Semite. He saw a Jew, come on, give him ten lashes on the pole. And then the porter hit him. And suddenly, he came to me-- I don't know why. He was a Polack, you know, Polack-German.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're going because they come call. You know is call? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The burning, no, the-- not called-- I went-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Wait, wait, wait. Mr. Dortheimer is just getting up to look at a dictionary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me just put this on you again, the microphone. I know the word. But I-- Coal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Coal.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said coal. A wagon of coals, and somebody must put out. You know-- come, come. You're going. I said, I'm not going. I am a painter. I-- they didn't come us to-- to transport your coal. They-- [NON-ENGLISH], Mr. [NON-ENGLISH], 10 lashes. This was the-- and this was the stairs. And he was, come, come. And I said, no, I'm not going. All right, I didn't care. I went up third floor, direct to director. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I knocked on the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you want? What? I said, director, they wanted to beat me. Who? And this-- this porter was already here. He said, excuse me, why you want to beat him? Because the engineer told us to beat him 10 lashes. I said, OK, call this-- tell the engineer to come to me. And the engineer came. What is it? He doesn't want to go to the coal. And the director said, excuse me, from today I am going to give him order, not you. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want something from him, you're coming to me. He went. And 14 days the director called him. And he was drunk. And said get out, in 14. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [LAUGHS] That was Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet any-- did you see any SS officers or anybody coming to give inspections to the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was giving what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any of the SS officers coming to inspect the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was funny story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't like to speak official to you. '44-- middle of '44, they liquidate-- the German-- liquidate every camps, what wasn't necessary for the German. When they don't recognize as a camp, they shot everyone. And to us, '44, in the middle, our Schindler was excited. And he said, my order-- everybody must do what's possible, quicker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Came a commission from the center, you know this? Center, the main office from concentration camp from Oranienburg. And they was checking this factory is OK. If not, they're gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Schindler was excited. If they don't recognize us as necessary, they're going to liquidate us. But Schindler factory, they got beautiful secretaries, five, six, young beautiful German. And when the officers, four officers came, immediately four secretary upstairs, food, drink. After four hours, they were drunk with passes. Schindler, OK, we are concentration camp now. And they leave us. They recognize us as necessary. This was Schindler.
